costs: Transition costs for the subset of schools currently offering 6-year programmes (fewer than full-system reform, but nontrivial)
Potential demand shift toward 8-year entry (age 12) as families seek the earliest remaining selection point, partially offsetting inequality-reduction goal
Negotiation costs with church/private maintainers of affected 6-year programmes [F-live-legal_and_governance-03]
content.equity_impact: Partial positive impact: removes the youngest and most extreme selection point, but since between-school stratification (not within-school homogeneity) is identified as the primary mechanism of early tracking's inequality effect regardless of specific age, and displaced demand may simply shift to the remaining age-12 entry, the overall segregation reduction is uncertain [F-live-international_comparison-06, F-live-finnish_reform-02].
expected_benefits: Targets the single earliest and most extreme selection point while preserving political and institutional continuity for 8-year and 4-year tracks
Reduces scope of the selection funnel without full system restructuring, potentially lowering implementation disruption relative to full delay-to-14 reform
Responds to evidence that earliest tracking ages are most strongly linked to inequality [F-live-finnish_reform-04]
content.goal: Eliminate the earliest and most controversial selection point (age 10, Grade 5 entry) while preserving the age-12 (Grade 7) and age-14 entry points, as an incremental structural compromise.

implementation_steps.action: Legislate sunset of new Grade-5 (age-10) gimnázium admissions cohorts, grandfathering enrolled students
implementation_steps.timeline: Legislative passage within 1-2 years; phase-out over 6 years for existing cohorts

implementation_steps.action: Convert 6-year programme capacity into 8-year or 4-year programme capacity, prioritizing schools with strong Grade-7 entry demand
implementation_steps.actor: Klebelsberg Centre and affected school maintainers

implementation_steps.action: Negotiate with church/private maintainers operating 6-year programmes on transition terms

mechanisms: Removing the earliest entry point shortens the selection funnel from three stages to two, targeting the point most associated with inequality per cross-national evidence [F-live-finnish_reform-04, F-live-portuguese_reform-01]

6-year track entry occurs at the youngest age, where selection-independent SES effects are least likely to reflect stable ability signals, given panel data showing score-evolution effects only observable from grade 8 onward [F-live-demography-03]

Partial reform reduces political conflict scope relative to full abolition, potentially avoiding the sweeping-transformation framing seen in response to broader admissions review proposals [F-live-political_feasibility-04]
risks: Displaced demand may concentrate more intensely at the age-12 entry point, potentially increasing competition and stakes for that remaining early track without net segregation reduction

Opposition may still frame partial reform as precedent for further structural change, replicating the political conflict seen with narrower admissions-review proposals [F-live-political_feasibility-04]

Segregation mechanism (school-level correlation between disadvantage and Roma share) may persist largely unchanged if 8-year and 4-year tracks retain similar selection dynamics [F-live-finnish_reform-03]
content.title: Cap and phase down 6-year (age-10) entry only, retain 8-year and 4-year tracks
